message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation files: src/cli.ts (NEW) and src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 44 (ALL tests now pass; 0 todo)
Recent Green phase: Created src/cli.ts — reads stdin (readFileSync(0, "utf8")), JSON.parse, runScenario, writes JSON.stringify(result) to stdout; try/catch writes the error message to stderr and process.exit(1) on failure.

This is the FINAL feature. After you finish refactoring and confirm all 44 tests are green, the orchestrator will write experiment-done.txt — but YOU should NOT write it.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Look at BOTH files holistically now that the implementation is complete: src/cli.ts (small I/O wrapper) and src/claim-office.ts (the full domain logic). Evaluate naming, Four Rules of Simple Design, any lingering smells or duplication across the whole module. Run ESLint. Measure APP mass / McCabe / cognitive before and after for anything you change. Apply genuine metric-improving cleanups; otherwise document why none are warranted. Keep whichever scores better.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
PRE-measurement results:
- **Tests**: 44/44 passing
- **ESLint smells**: 0 across both files (clean surface)
- **Cognitive complexity**: no violations reported (all functions below the SonarJS threshold)

ESLint is clean. Now I'll compute APP mass and McCabe for the functions to find any remaining target. Let me focus on the larger functions.

**McCabe analysis (per function):**

- `runScenario` (orchestrator): start 1; `step.op === "quote"` if +1; `quoteCount > 0` ternary +1 → **McCabe 3**. This is the highest in the file but still low.
- `reimbursableAmount`: start 1; one `if` +1 → McCabe 2
- `componentBasePremium`: start 1; ternary +1 → McCabe 2
- `validateDamageCounts`: start 1; `for` +1; `if` +1 → McCabe 3
- `validateQuoteItems`: start 1; `for` +1; `if` +1 → McCabe 3
- All the small predicate/helper functions: McCabe 1.

No function exceeds McCabe 3. The code is already heavily decomposed into small, well-named single-purpose functions.

**Naming evaluation** (first priority): Names are precise and intent-revealing — `quotePremium`, `curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`, `reimbursableAmount`, `desiredPayout`, `validateDamageCounts`. The constants are named (`DEDUCTIBLE`, `CAP_MULTIPLIER`, `HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCH_THRESHOLD`). No magic numbers. `isComponent`/`isMainItem` read clearly.

Let me look for any genuine duplication or smells. One candidate: `mainItemBase` is just `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]`, and `basePremium` accesses `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` directly via `(sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]`. Let me check if that's worth unifying, and examine the `step`/`index`/`policies[index]` coupling.

Let me verify the one duplication candidate is real.

content.content:
110:    .reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type], 0);
114:const mainItemBase = (item: Item): number => BASE_PREMIUM[item.type];

content.text:
There is a small conceptual duplication: `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` is accessed both inline at line 110 and via the named helper `mainItemBase` at line 114. The named helper `mainItemBase` reveals intent better than the raw subscript. Applying **Rule 3 (No Duplication)** by routing line 110 through `mainItemBase` removes the duplicated knowledge access and improves intent at the call site.

Let me measure the target function `basePremium` PRE:

**`basePremium` PRE (lines 107-112):**
```
const mainTotal = items.filter(isMainItem).reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type], 0);
return mainTotal + componentTotal(items);
```
- Constants: 1 (the `0`)
- Bindings: `items`, `mainTotal`, `sum`, `item` = 4
- Invocations: `filter`, `isMainItem`, `reduce`, `+` (sum+...), `BASE_PREMIUM[]` subscript ~ count as invocation, `+` (mainTotal+...), `componentTotal` ≈ 7
- Conditionals: 0; Loops: 1 (`reduce`); Assignments: 0

This is a one-for-one swap (replace the subscript expression with a call to the equally-massed `mainItemBase` invocation) — APP mass and McCabe stay identical, but it removes duplicated knowledge (DRY) and raises intent at the call site. This is a clarity/duplication win at neutral metric cost, exactly the Rule 2/Rule 3 case the discipline permits.

content.text:
POST-measurement confirmed: 44/44 tests green, 0 ESLint smells.

I reviewed the rest of the module for further metric-improving changes and found none warranted:

- **`runScenario` (McCabe 3, highest in file)** — the `step.op === "quote"` branch dispatches between quote and claim handling, and the `quoteCount > 0` ternary derives the follow-up flag. Both branches are essential domain decisions; extracting them would add invocation mass without removing any decision point (McCabe would stay 3) and would split the policy-index/quote-count state across functions, hurting clarity. Kept.
- **`cli.ts`** — a minimal I/O wrapper: read stdin, parse, run, write; `try/catch` handles failure. McCabe 2 (one catch), no smells. Nothing to simplify without losing the error path. Kept.
- **Constants and predicates** — all named, no magic numbers, McCabe 1 each. At their floor.

Only one genuine cleanup applied.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring**: Routed `basePremium`'s main-item subtotal through the existing `mainItemBase` helper instead of duplicating the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` table access inline (Rule 3 — No Duplication; Rule 2 — Reveals Intent at the call site). Also moved `mainItemBase` above its first use.

**Pre/Post deltas (function `basePremium`)**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (already clean module-wide)
- Cognitive complexity: below threshold → below threshold (unchanged)
- APP mass: neutral — one invocation (`mainItemBase(item)`) replaces one subscript invocation (`BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]`), same component cost
- McCabe cyclomatic: 1 → 1 (no decision points either way)
- Duplication: removed — `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` now has a single representation via `mainItemBase`

**Tests**: 44/44 passing.

The change is metric-neutral on APP/McCabe but eliminates conceptual duplication and raises intent, which Rule 3 prioritizes over the (unchanged) complexity scores — so the refactored version is kept. The rest of `src/claim-office.ts` and `src/cli.ts` were already at or near their metric floors with precise naming, so no further changes were warranted.

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/cli.ts`
